ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Of the Council at Sardica.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The Western prelates on account of their being of another language, and not understanding this exposition, would not admit of it; saying that the Nicene Creed was sufficient, and that they would not waste time on anything beyond it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But when the emperor had again written to insist on the restoration to Paul and Athanasius of their respective sees, but without effect in consequence of the continual agitation of the people—these two bishops demanded that another Synod should be convened, so that their case, as well as other questions in relation to the faith might be settled by an ecumenical council, for they made it obvious that their deposition arose from no other cause than that the faith might be the more easily perverted.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Another general council was therefore summoned to meet at Sardica,—a city of Illyricum,—by the joint authority of the two emperors; the one requesting by letter that it might be so, and the other, of the East, readily acquiescing in it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It was the eleventh year after the death of the father of the two Augusti, during the consulship of Rufinus and Eusebius, that the Synod of Sardica met.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: According to the statement of Athanasius about 300 bishops from the western parts of the empire were present; but Sabinus says there came only seventy from the eastern parts, among whom was Ischyras of Mareotes, who had been ordained bishop of that country by those who deposed Athanasius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of the rest, some pretended infirmity of body; others complained of the shortness of the notice given, casting the blame of it on Julius, bishop of Rome, although a year and a half had elapsed from the time of its having been summoned: in which interval Athanasius remained at Rome awaiting the assembling of the Synod.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: When at last they were convened at Sardica, the Eastern prelates refused either to meet or to enter into any conference with those of the West, unless they first excluded Athanasius and Paul from the convention.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But as Protogenes, bishop of Sardica, and Hosius, bishop of Cordova, a city in Spain, would by no means permit them to be absent, the Eastern bishops immediately withdrew, and returning to Philippopolis in Thrace, held a separate council, wherein they openly anathematized the term homoousios; and having introduced the Anomoian opinion into their epistles, they sent them in all directions.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On the other hand those who remained at Sardica, condemning in the first place their departure, afterwards divested the accusers of Athanasius of their dignity; then confirming the Nicene Creed, and rejecting the term anomoion, they more distinctly recognized the doctrine of consubstantiality, which they also inserted in epistles addressed to all the churches.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Both parties believed they had acted rightly: those of the East, because the Western bishops had countenanced those whom they had deposed; and these again, in consequence not only of the retirement of those who had deposed them before the matter had been examined into, but also because they themselves were the defenders of the Nicene faith, which the other party had dared to adulterate.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They therefore restored to Paul and Athanasius their sees, and also Marcellus of Ancyra in Lesser Galatia, who had been deposed long before, as we have stated in the former book.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: At that time indeed he exerted himself to the utmost to procure the revocation of the sentence pronounced against him, declaring that his being suspected of entertaining the error of Paul of Samosata arose from a misunderstanding of some expressions in his book.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It must, however, be noticed that Eusebius Pamphilus wrote three entire books against Marcellus, in which he quotes that author’s own words to prove that he asserts with Sabellius the Libyan, and Paul of Samosata, that the Lord [Jesus] was a mere man.
